what_it_does: This file is a human-readable metadata registry for MCP tools. It maps each machine tool name to friendly presentation data—human name, plain-English description, operation labels, ordered visible parameters, parameter labels, and live enum sources—that /api/tools joins onto the underlying machine descriptor to produce a unified, user-facing tool descriptor.
observations: One entry per tool, keyed by the machine tool name.
Some tools carry rich operation metadata (op_labels, op_params, param_labels, enum_sources), while many others leave those objects empty.
enum_sources link specific parameters to live registry endpoints such as /api/layers to populate dropdowns.
A header note documents the design intent: authored human layer, degrade-clean fallbacks, registry-is-truth for enums, and separation from bridge/client code.
Several entries explicitly state they are not run from the tool list (node, connect, set_config, run_graph, cc_clone, cc_gate, session_post, self_change_log, channel_act).
human_description values are written as plain-English UX copy rather than raw docstrings.
op_params arrays define the visible parameter order and intentionally omit expert knobs.
The registry covers roughly fifty tools spanning memory, sessions, workspaces, reasoning steps, pipelines, voice, channels, and noticeboards.
standouts: It is a deliberately decoupled presentation layer: friendly copy lives here, machine schema lives elsewhere.
The degrade-clean contract—missing tool yields form_meta=null, dead enum source falls back to free-text—is stated explicitly.
enum_sources delegate option lists to live endpoints instead of hardcoding values.
A cluster of entries are marked as display-only or protected-surface actions rather than runnable tools.
conventions: Top-level _schema and _note fields document version and intent.
Tool names are machine identifiers; human_name is the friendly label.
op_params arrays preserve an explicit visible parameter order.
Empty objects are used when a tool has no operations or no parameters to expose.
Descriptions are written in second-person, task-oriented language.
concerns: Completeness is inconsistent: some tools have full op metadata, others have none, with no schema-level indication of why.
enum_sources only cover a few parameters; there is no documented fallback label or cache behavior if the endpoint is unreachable.
Tools that are not runnable from the tool list are still included, which could confuse consumers that treat the registry as an action catalog.
There is no per-tool versioning or last-edited metadata beyond the global _schema.
Some descriptions reference on-screen UI concepts that may not apply to all consumers of /api/tools.
No explicit locale or internationalization structure is present.
notes: This is the curated friendly face for the MCP tool registry: it turns machine tool names into plain-English labels and operation guides while staying intentionally separate from the underlying schema.
questions: How exactly does /api/tools perform the join with the FastMCP machine descriptor?
What criteria determine which parameters are hidden as expert knobs versus listed in op_params?
How are enum_sources authenticated, cached, and handled when the source is slow or unavailable?
Are human_names used as display titles, tool selectors, or both?
What process updates this file when new tools are added or existing tool schemas change?
Why include tools that are explicitly not runnable from the tool list in the same registry?
Is there a separate review/approval trail for the authored content mentioned as lead/Tim refined?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is exactly the human-readable companion registry described in the header, keyed by tool name, intended to join with machine descriptors at /api/tools and to degrade cleanly when data is missing.
apparent_themes: Separation of human presentation from machine schema
Graceful degradation when metadata or enum sources are absent
Curated, authored UX copy over generated documentation
Surface simplification by hiding expert parameters
intention_signals: The file appears designed to give a UI or conversational surface a stable, human-curated way to render MCP tools, operations, and parameters in plain language, while keeping that presentation layer insulated from code changes and machine schema drift.
novelty: The explicit degrade-clean contract and the enum_sources pattern—delegating dropdown values to live registry endpoints rather than hardcoding options—stand out as unusual for a tool metadata file.
contribution: This file contributes a curated human presentation layer for MCP tools: friendly names, plain-English descriptions, operation labels, ordered visible parameters, parameter labels, and live enum sources that a fused system would keep so tool surfaces do not have to rely solely on raw agent docstrings or machine schemas.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON registry mapping MCP tool names to human-friendly names, descriptions, operation labels, ordered visible parameters, parameter labels, and live enum-source URLs for the /api/tools surface.
intention_for_embedding: Intended to provide the human presentation layer that joins onto machine tool descriptors so surfaces can display tools in plain English with curated operations and inputs.
